items.q: How do you say 'I do not eat' in French?
items.why: Students forget the verb 'sandwich'. Ne and pas wrap AROUND the verb in present tense - ne BEFORE the verb, pas AFTER. In compound tenses, they wrap around the auxiliary.
Sensible is a faux ami. French 'sensible' = sensitive/easily affected. English 'sensible' = raisonnable in French. Confusion leads to wrong impressions of someone's character.

items.q: How do you describe weather in the past, e.g. 'it was sunny'?
items.misconception: Passé composé - 'Il a fait beau' (which suggests a completed event).
items.why: Weather descriptions in past narratives use IMPERFECT (ongoing state/description). Passé composé is for completed events. 'Quand je suis arrivé, il faisait beau' = ongoing weather backdrop; arrived = completed action.
Correct answer: Evening. “Morning” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's After. “Before” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many parts of day are listed?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
